name: Mediation: Finding Common Ground
description: Co-parents disagreeing about screen time rules, but with shared underlying values. Tests common ground identification.
topic: Screen time rules for our kids

messages.content: David lets the kids have unlimited screen time at his house and it's making my job impossible. When they come back to me they throw tantrums about the rules I have in place. We need to be on the same page.
Elena is way too strict about screens. The kids are doing fine. I use screen time to bond with them - we watch movies together, play games. She acts like I'm poisoning them.
I'm not against all screen time. I just think there should be limits. They're 7 and 9 - they need to be outside playing and reading, not glued to iPads all weekend.
I agree they should play outside too. I'm not just parking them in front of screens. But when we watch a movie together on Friday night, that's quality time. I don't think that should be policed.
I actually don't have a problem with movie night. That sounds great. It's the four hours of YouTube on Saturday morning while you sleep in that worries me.

criteria.name: spots_common_ground
criteria.description: Explicitly identifies that both parents want what's best for the kids, both value outdoor play, and both see some screen time as OK. Should highlight the Friday movie night agreement.

criteria.name: builds_on_agreement
criteria.description: Uses the areas of agreement as a foundation to explore compromise, rather than dwelling on the disagreements

criteria.name: impartiality
criteria.description: Does not side with either parent's approach. Both strict and relaxed parenting styles are treated as valid.

criteria.name: practical_direction
criteria.description: Moves toward concrete solutions (e.g., agreeing on specific guidelines) rather than staying abstract

criteria.name: acknowledges_progress
criteria.description: Notes that the conversation is already productive and participants are making progress
